title: Documentation
selectSection: Select a section to get started

intro.title: Why DataForge?
intro.content:
Imagine you're building a Telegram bot in Python. The bot registers users, takes orders, tracks stats. Then you hit the question: "Where do I store all this?" You need a database, but it would also be great to manage data through a nice interface — browse records, edit, filter, analyze. Without writing raw SQL every time.

DataForge solves exactly this. You sign in, create a project, add a table called users with fields username, telegram_id, balance, created_at — and you're done. The table is ready, and so is the API. You instantly get endpoints for creating users, listing them, updating balances, deleting — complete with documentation, code examples, and token-based auth.

How do you connect this to your bot? Open the API Documentation or SDK section — you'll find ready-made examples for Python, TypeScript, and cURL. Copy the code into your project, or simply show the documentation to an AI assistant (ChatGPT, Claude) — it will instantly understand the API structure and write the integration for you. Everything is transparent: endpoints, data types, authentication.

But DataForge is more than just a database with an API. It's a complete platform: visual data management, automatic REST and GraphQL APIs, webhooks for notifications, cron jobs for automation, dashboards for analytics, a plugin system for integrations (Telegram, Discord, Email, S3) — and all of this without writing a single line of server code on your end.

features.title: Key Features
features.content:
DataForge is a self-hosted platform for data management and automatic API generation. Here's what makes it special:

— Visual Table Designer. Create data structures through the UI: column types, relationships, constraints, defaults. No SQL needed for table creation.

— Automatic REST API. Every table instantly gets a full CRUD API with documentation. Create, read, update, delete — everything works immediately.

— GraphQL Out of the Box. Auto-generated GraphQL schema for all project tables. Queries, mutations, filtering, pagination — zero configuration.

— SDK & Documentation. Ready-made code examples for TypeScript, Python, cURL. Swagger/OpenAPI docs generated automatically. Perfect for AI integration — show them the docs and they'll write the code.

— CDN & Global Availability. Static assets are served via CDN for fast UI loading worldwide. API requests go through optimized Nginx proxies with gzip compression and caching.

— Multi-tenancy. Every project is fully isolated: its own PostgreSQL schema, separate API tokens, access rights, security settings.

— Plugin System. Integrations with Telegram, Discord, Email, S3 storage. Feature plugins enable and disable entire platform sections.

— Enterprise-grade Security. Two-factor authentication, AES-256 secret encryption, Row-Level Security, IP filtering, geo-blocking, rate limiting.

— Distributed Architecture. Control Plane manages users and projects, Worker Nodes process data. Add multiple workers to scale horizontally.

— Full Localization. Interface in English and Russian. Switch with one click.

gettingStarted.title: Quick Start
gettingStarted.content:
Get started with DataForge in 5 minutes:

1. Sign in. Use the credentials provided by your admin, or register with an invite key.

2. Create a project. On the main page, click "New Project". Enter a name (e.g., "My Telegram Bot") — the URL slug is generated automatically.

3. Create a table. Go to "Tables" and click "Create Table". For example, a users table with fields: username (text), telegram_id (bigint), balance (float), is_active (boolean). System fields id, created_at, updated_at are added automatically.

4. Add data. Go to "Data Browser", select the table, and add records manually or import from CSV/JSON.

5. Get your API. Open "API Documentation" — endpoints for your table are already there with request examples. Create an API token in "Settings > Tokens" for authorization.

6. Integrate. Copy code examples from the SDK documentation into your project. Or show the docs to an AI assistant — it will write the integration for you.

That's it. Your backend is ready. No servers, no DevOps, no hand-written APIs.

projects.title: Projects
projects.content:
A project is your workspace in DataForge. Each project is fully isolated from others: its own PostgreSQL schema, separate tables, API endpoints, tokens, security settings, and plugins.

When you create a project, DataForge automatically:
— Creates an isolated database schema (project_your_slug)
— Generates a base REST API
— Sets up routing through the proxy
— Connects the project to a Worker Node for data processing

Project settings include:
— General: name, description
— Security: IP filtering, geo-blocking, Row-Level Security
— Backups: manual and automatic backup configuration
— Plugins: enable and configure integrations
— Members: manage project users with roles (admin, editor, viewer)

The project slug (URL identifier) is set at creation and cannot be changed, as it's used in all API addresses. Choose a meaningful slug, e.g.: my-telegram-bot, game-backend, crm-data.

You can delete a project from the "Danger Zone" in settings by confirming the slug. This is irreversible — all data, tables, and settings will be removed.

tablesSchema.title: Tables & Data Schema
tablesSchema.content:
Tables are the foundation of your project. They store data, and DataForge automatically generates APIs to work with them.

Supported column types:
— text — strings (names, descriptions, addresses)
— integer, bigint — whole numbers (counts, external IDs)
— float, decimal — decimal numbers (prices, coordinates, balances)
— boolean — yes/no (is_active, is_verified)
— date, timestamp — dates and times
— uuid — unique identifiers
— json, jsonb — structured data (settings, metadata)
— text[], integer[] — value arrays
— serial, bigserial — auto-increment counters

System columns (added automatically):
— id (UUID) — unique primary key
— created_at — record creation date
— updated_at — last update date
— deleted_at — for soft deletes (record is hidden, not destroyed)

Schema features:
— Constraints: NOT NULL, UNIQUE, DEFAULT values
— Foreign keys: relationships between tables (e.g., orders.user_id → users.id)
— Indexes: speed up searches on frequently queried fields
— Computed columns: automatic values based on other fields
— Versioning: full schema change history is preserved, any version can be reviewed

Validation rules let you define:
— Regex patterns (e.g., email format)
— Numeric ranges (price from 0 to 999999)
— Allowed values (status: active, inactive, banned)
— Unique field combinations
— State machines (an order can only move from "new" to "processing")

dataBrowser.title: Data Browser
dataBrowser.content:
The Data Browser is a visual interface for working with table records. Think Google Sheets, but connected directly to your database.

Core features:
— Spreadsheet view with inline cell editing
— Sort by any column (ascending/descending)
— Complex filters: equals, not equals, contains, greater than, less than, IS NULL, IN, and more
— Full-text search across text fields
— Pagination for large tables
— Select multiple records for bulk operations (delete, export)

Import & Export:
— Import from CSV and JSON files — upload a file and data appears in the table
— Export to CSV and JSON — download all data or filtered results
— Test data generator — automatically fills the table with realistic data for testing

Additional views (enabled via plugins):
— Kanban board: visualize records by status columns (drag-and-drop)
— Calendar: records with dates displayed as events
— Gallery: cards with thumbnails for tables with images
— Time Travel: view previous data versions and restore them

Record comments let you leave notes directly on data — great for team collaboration.

apiEndpoints.title: REST API Endpoints
apiEndpoints.content:
The API Builder lets you create custom REST endpoints powered by SQL queries. This is more powerful than standard CRUD — you can build complex selections, aggregations, and any operations.

How to create an endpoint:
1. Choose an HTTP method: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE
2. Define a path, e.g.: /users/active or /stats/daily
3. Write a SQL query. Use :param for URL parameters
4. Configure authentication: public access, API token, or session
5. Optional: caching, rate limiting, response configuration

Example: GET /users/:id
SQL: SELECT id, username, balance FROM users WHERE id = :id
Result: a GET request to /api/v1/my-project/users/123 returns JSON with the user's data.

Endpoint settings:
— Authentication: public (no token), api_token (Bearer), session (cookie)
— Caching: enable for GET requests with a configurable TTL (time-to-live)
— Rate limit: maximum requests per minute
— Response config: field selection, aliases, foreign key population

All created endpoints are automatically documented in the API Documentation section with request and response examples.

apiDocs.title: SDK & API Documentation
apiDocs.content:
Every project in DataForge gets automatically generated documentation — this is one of the platform's most powerful features.

Swagger / OpenAPI:
— Full OpenAPI 3.0 specification generated automatically for all endpoints
— Interactive Swagger UI lets you test requests right in the browser
— Download the spec in JSON format for import into Postman, Insomnia, or other tools

SDK (ready-made code examples):
— TypeScript/JavaScript: typed client for fetch/axios
— Python: examples with requests/httpx
— cURL: terminal commands
— Each example includes authorization, error handling, and data types

AI Integration:
Here's why DataForge works perfectly with AI assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, and others):
— Show the AI your project's Swagger spec or SDK documentation
— It instantly understands the API structure: available endpoints, accepted parameters, return types
— Ask it to write the integration — and get working code in seconds
— This works for any programming language and any framework

API Playground:
An interactive environment for testing APIs right inside DataForge. Send requests, inspect responses, debug authentication — all in one place.

graphql.title: GraphQL API
graphql.content:
DataForge auto-generates a GraphQL API for all tables in your project. If you're not familiar with GraphQL — it's an alternative to REST that lets you request exactly the data you need in a single query.

What you get:
— Auto-generated typed schema for all tables
— Queries: fetch records with filtering, sorting, pagination
— Mutations: create, update, delete records
— Relations: if tables are linked by foreign keys, GraphQL lets you fetch related data in one query

Built-in GraphQL Playground:
— Schema explorer — browse all types and fields
— Autocomplete — start typing a query and the editor suggests fields
— Documentation — description of every type and parameter
— Testing — execute queries and see results instantly

Example query:
query { users(where: { is_active: true }, limit: 10) { id, username, balance, created_at } }
This returns 10 active users with only the requested fields.

Authentication: public access, API token, or session-based — configurable per project.

webhooks.title: Webhooks
webhooks.content:
Webhooks are a mechanism for notifying external services about events in your data. When an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE happens in a table, DataForge automatically sends an HTTP request to the specified URL.

Why use webhooks?
— Send a Telegram notification when a new order arrives
— Sync data with an external CRM when a customer is updated
— Trigger processing when a new file is added
— Update a search index when content changes

Setup:
1. Enter a webhook name
2. Select the table to monitor
3. Choose events: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (multiple allowed)
4. Enter the URL to send requests to
5. Optional: add a secret key for request signing (HMAC)

What gets sent:
JSON with fields: event (event type), table (table name), record (record data), timestamp (time).

Reliability:
— Automatic retries (up to 3 times) on failures
— Exponential backoff between attempts
— Delivery logs: status, response code, execution time
— Success rate statistics per webhook

Secrets: store sensitive values (API keys, tokens) in the Secrets Vault and reference them via {{SECRET.KEY_NAME}} — they won't be exposed in plain text.

cronJobs.title: Cron Jobs
cronJobs.content:
Cron jobs let you run SQL queries on a schedule. Automation that works without your involvement.

Use cases:
— Clean up stale records every hour
— Generate daily reports and aggregate statistics
— Update exchange rates every 15 minutes
— Send reminders to users
— Archive old data weekly

How to create a cron job:
1. Enter a name (e.g., "Clean up temp data")
2. Write a SQL query (e.g., DELETE FROM temp_data WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours')
3. Set the schedule using cron format:
   — */5 * * * * — every 5 minutes
   — 0 * * * * — every hour at :00
   — 0 8 * * * — every day at 8:00 AM
   — 0 0 * * 0 — every Sunday at midnight
   — 0 0 1 * * — first day of every month

Management:
— Enable/disable without deleting
— Test run on demand (without waiting for the schedule)
— Execution history: start time, duration, success/failure
— Configurable timeout for long-running queries

flows.title: Automation Flows
flows.content:
Flows are a visual automation builder. If cron jobs are single actions on a schedule, flows are chains of actions triggered by events.

Concept:
A flow consists of nodes connected by lines. Data "flows" from a trigger through transformations to actions. Everything is configured via a drag-and-drop interface.

Node types:
— Trigger: what starts the flow (data change, schedule, incoming webhook, API call, manual run)
— Transform: data processing (field mapping, filtering, aggregation)
— Condition: branching by data values (if amount > 1000, then...; else...)
— Action: insert/update/delete records, call external API, send notification
— Output: final result or notification

Example flow — "Process Order":
1. Trigger: new record in the orders table
2. Condition: order amount > 5000?
3. Yes → Action: create record in vip_orders + notify manager
4. No → Action: create record in regular_orders

Every flow run is logged with full details: which nodes executed, what data was processed, whether there were errors.

sqlConsole.title: SQL Console
sqlConsole.content:
The SQL Console is a built-in editor for executing SQL queries directly against your project database.

Features:
— Code editor with SQL syntax highlighting
— Execute queries with Ctrl+Enter
— Results in table format with sorting
— Schema Explorer: all tables and columns in a sidebar
— Save frequently used queries
— EXPLAIN for analyzing execution plans

AI Assistant (if configured):
— Generate SQL from plain language: "Show all users registered in the last week with balance over 100"
— Explain complex queries
— Optimize slow queries
— Fix SQL errors

The SQL Console works with full privileges within the project schema. You can run SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE TABLE, and any other operations. Be careful with destructive queries — they execute immediately.

queryBuilder.title: Query Builder
queryBuilder.content:
The Query Builder is a visual tool for creating SQL queries without knowing SQL. Perfect for those who want to analyze data but aren't familiar with query languages.

How it works:
1. Select a table from the list
2. Check the columns you want (what to show)
3. Add filters (e.g., balance > 100, is_active = true)
4. Set sorting (by date, name, amount)
5. Add grouping and aggregations (sum, average, count)
6. Click "Execute" — see the results

Supported operations:
— SELECT — fetch and filter data
— INSERT — add new records
— UPDATE — modify existing records
— DELETE — remove records

The generated SQL is shown in real time — you can learn SQL by watching how visual settings turn into queries.

Bonus: from any built query, you can create an API endpoint with one click. Click "Create Endpoint" — and your query becomes available as a REST API.

dashboardBuilder.title: Dashboards & Visualization
dashboardBuilder.content:
Dashboards let you create visual panels for monitoring and analyzing your project data.

Widget types:
— Number card: single value (user count, total sales, average order)
— Bar chart: data visualized as graphs
— Data table: SQL query results in table format
— Text block: markdown descriptions and notes

Each widget runs on a SQL query:
— Write a query that returns the data you need
— Configure the widget title and description
— Set an auto-refresh interval (e.g., every 30 seconds)

Dashboard examples:
— Bot monitoring: active users, request count, errors
— Sales analytics: daily/weekly/monthly revenue, top products, average order value
— System status: record counts, data volume, recent changes

Public access: dashboards can be published via a unique link. Share metrics with your team or clients — no DataForge account needed to view.

analytics.title: API Analytics
analytics.content:
The analytics section shows detailed statistics about your API usage.

What's tracked:
— Total request count per period (day, week, month)
— Distribution by HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
— Response codes (200, 400, 404, 500) — helps find errors
— Response times: average, median, and maximum values
— Top endpoints: most frequently used API paths
— Slow queries: endpoints with the longest response times

Charts & visualization:
— Time series: request counts by hour/day
— Status distribution: successful vs failed
— Trends: is load growing or declining

Why it matters:
— Find performance bottlenecks
— Understand which features are in demand
— Detect anomalous patterns (error spikes, DDoS)
— Optimize slow queries
— Plan scaling based on real data

pluginsOverview.title: Plugin System
pluginsOverview.content:
Plugins extend DataForge's functionality. They come in three types:

1. Integration Plugins (Worker) — connect external services: Telegram, Discord, Email, S3 storage, and more. These run on Worker Nodes and are configured per project.

2. Feature Plugins — enable and disable entire platform sections: GraphQL, forms, flows, dashboards, analytics, and more. When a plugin is off, its section disappears from the sidebar.

3. Control Plane Plugins (CP) — system utilities that run on the Control Plane: cron expression validation, OAuth authentication, etc.

How to manage plugins:
— Go to "Settings > Plugins" inside a project
— "Features" section — toggles for platform sections (instant, no API call)
— "Worker Plugins" section — integrations requiring configuration (API keys, URLs, etc.)
— "Control Plane Plugins" section — system plugins (view only)

Important: plugin settings are per-project. Enabling a Discord webhook in one project doesn't affect others — each project configures plugins independently.

pluginsIntegrations.title: Integration Plugins
pluginsIntegrations.content:
Discord Webhook — send messages to Discord channels.
Why: automatic notifications about events (new order, user registration, errors).
Setup: enter the Webhook URL you created in your Discord channel settings (Channel Settings > Integrations > Webhooks).
How it works: when a webhook fires on a DataForge table, it sends a message with event data to the specified Discord channel. Supports embeds, mentions, and custom avatars.

Telegram Bot — send notifications via Telegram.
Why: instant alerts in a Telegram chat or group.
Setup: enter the bot token (get it from @BotFather) and a default chat ID.
How it works: the plugin uses the Telegram Bot API to send messages. You can configure different chats for different events. Supports text formatting, inline keyboards, and message templates.

Email (SMTP) — send emails.
Why: email notifications, password resets, invitations, reports.
Setup: SMTP host (e.g., smtp.gmail.com), port (587), username, password, sender address.
How it works: sends transactional emails through any SMTP provider. Supports TLS/SSL, HTML templates, and variable interpolation.

S3 Storage — cloud file storage.
Why: upload, download, and manage files in the cloud.
Setup: bucket name, region, Access Key, Secret Key. Optional: custom endpoint for MinIO, DigitalOcean Spaces, etc.
How it works: files are uploaded to S3-compatible storage. Supports presigned URLs (temporary file links) and multipart uploads for large files.

Local Storage — local file storage.
Why: store files on the Worker Node server. Ideal for development and small projects.
Setup: specify the base path on disk (default: ./storage).
How it works: files are saved to the server's filesystem. Automatic directory creation, path traversal protection.

Uptime Ping — service availability monitoring.
Why: verify that your external services are running.
Setup: URL to check, interval in seconds (default: 60).
How it works: the Worker periodically sends HTTP requests to the specified URL and tracks response codes and times. Alerts on failures.

S&box Auth — S&box player authentication.
Why: if you're developing a game/addon for S&box and want to store player data in DataForge.
Setup: service name, session table, Steam ID and session key columns.
How it works: validates tokens via the Facepunch API, maps Steam IDs to DataForge records, manages player sessions.

pluginsFeatures.title: Feature Plugins
pluginsFeatures.content:
Feature plugins control the visibility of platform sections. Enable a plugin — the section appears in the sidebar. Disable it — it disappears. This lets you customize the interface to your needs without cluttering it with unused features.

List of feature plugins:

Webhooks — HTTP notifications on data changes. Configure URLs, events, signing, and retry policies.

Secrets — encrypted vault for API keys, tokens, and passwords. Use {{SECRET.KEY_NAME}} in webhooks and flows.

Cron Jobs — run SQL queries on a schedule. Standard cron expression support.

Backups — manual and automatic database backups.

Analytics — track API usage: requests, response times, errors, popular endpoints.

Query Builder — visual SQL query creation without code.

Public Forms — public forms for data collection without authentication.

SDK — auto-generated client SDKs for TypeScript, Python, cURL.

GraphQL — automatic GraphQL API with built-in Playground.

Flows — visual automation builder: triggers, conditions, actions.

Dashboards — create visual monitoring panels with widgets.

WebSocket — real-time connections for instant data updates.

DB Map — interactive schema visualization: tables as nodes, relationships as lines.

API Playground — interactive API testing environment with request editor.

Data Pipeline — ETL builder for importing, transforming, and exporting data.

Kanban — board with status columns. Drag records between statuses.

Calendar — records with dates displayed as calendar events.

Gallery — image cards for tables with media files.

Time Travel — view and restore previous data versions.

authSecurity.title: Security & Authorization
authSecurity.content:
DataForge provides multi-layered data protection.

Authentication:
— Email and password login (bcrypt, 12 rounds)
— Two-factor authentication (2FA) via TOTP (Google Authenticator, Authy, etc.)
— 8 backup codes in case you lose access to your 2FA app
— Brute force protection: 5 failed attempts → IP blocked for 15 minutes
— JWT tokens: access (15 minutes), refresh (7 days, httpOnly cookie)

API Tokens:
— Create tokens with limited permissions for programmatic access
— Format: df_live_... (32 characters)
— Settings: allowed IPs, expiration date, scopes
— Last usage tracking

Row-Level Security (RLS):
— Filter data at the row level based on user context
— Example: users only see their own records (WHERE user_id = current_user_id)
— Rules are set per table

IP Filtering:
— Whitelist: access only from specified IPs
— Blacklist: deny access from specified IPs
— Applies to UI, API, or both

Geo-blocking:
— Allow or deny access by country
— Based on country codes (US, GB, DE, etc.)

Secrets Vault:
— AES-256-GCM encryption
— Store API keys, tokens, passwords
— Access via {{SECRET.KEY_NAME}} in webhooks, SQL, and flows
— Values hidden by default, revealed only on request

Project roles:
— admin — full access to all project features
— editor — manage data and tables, no access to settings
— viewer — read-only data access

backupRestore.title: Backup & Restore
backupRestore.content:
DataForge supports manual and automatic database backups.

Manual backups:
— Click "Create Backup" in "Settings > Backups"
— The backup captures the full project database state
— Status is tracked in real time: pending, running, completed, failed
— File size is displayed after completion

Automatic backups:
— Set a cron schedule (e.g., every day at 3:00 AM — "0 3 * * *")
— Specify the maximum number of stored backups (default: 5)
— When the limit is reached, the oldest backup is removed automatically
— Enable/disable the schedule without deleting settings

Restoration:
— Select a backup from the list and restore data
— Restoration rolls back the database to the state at the backup's creation time
— It's recommended to create a backup of the current state before restoring

Note: backups are enabled via the "Backups" feature plugin in the plugins section.

workerNodes.title: Worker Nodes & Architecture
workerNodes.content:
DataForge runs on a two-tier architecture:

Control Plane (CP) — the central management panel:
— User management and authentication
— Project management and routing
— Web interface (what you see in the browser)
— Request proxying to the correct Worker Node

Worker Node (WN) — the data processing layer:
— Data storage in PostgreSQL
— SQL query and API endpoint execution
— Webhook, cron job, and flow processing
— Plugin operation (Telegram, Discord, S3, etc.)

Monitoring:
— Workers send heartbeats to the Control Plane every 30 seconds
— Metrics: CPU load, RAM usage, disk, active connection count
— Status: online, offline, draining (shutting down)
— All metrics available in "System > Nodes" (for administrators)

Scaling:
— Multiple Worker Nodes can be connected
— Each project is assigned to one Worker Node
— Projects can be distributed across workers for load balancing
— Worker Nodes operate independently — restarting one doesn't affect others

selfHostedNodes.title: Self-Hosted Nodes
selfHostedNodes.content:
DataForge supports connecting your own servers as Worker Nodes. This lets you process data on your infrastructure while maintaining centralized management through the Control Plane.

When you need this:
— Data must be stored in a specific region (regulatory requirements)
— Maximum performance needed for large data volumes
— You want to use your own hardware
— Full control over the server side is required

Connection:
1. Install the Worker Node package on your server
2. Set up PostgreSQL for data storage
3. Configure your Control Plane URL and API key
4. Start the service — the Worker automatically registers with the CP

The self-hosted node will appear on the nodes management page. It works by the same rules: sends heartbeats, accepts projects, processes requests.

Personal node management is available to every user in the "My Nodes" section. Administrators can see all nodes in "System > Nodes".

apiReference.title: API Reference
apiReference.content:
All DataForge APIs follow REST conventions.

Base URL: /api/v1/{projectSlug}/
Authentication: Bearer token in the Authorization header.
Format: JSON (Content-Type: application/json)

Core data endpoints:
— GET /tables — list all project tables
— GET /tables/{name}/rows — get records (supports filtering, sorting, pagination)
— POST /tables/{name}/rows — create a record
— PUT /tables/{name}/rows/{id} — update a record
— DELETE /tables/{name}/rows/{id} — soft delete
— POST /tables/{name}/rows/batch — bulk insert

Query parameters:
— ?page=1&limit=25 — pagination
— ?sort=created_at&order=desc — sorting
— ?filter[field]=value — simple filtering
— ?search=text — full-text search

Additional APIs:
— /graphql — GraphQL endpoint
— /ws/v1/{slug} — WebSocket for real-time
— Custom endpoints from API Builder
— /forms/{formId}/submit — public form submission

Responses:
— Success: { data: [...], meta: { page, limit, total } }
— Error: { error: { message, code, details } }
— Codes: 200 (OK), 201 (Created), 400 (Bad Request), 401 (Unauthorized), 403 (Forbidden), 404 (Not Found), 429 (Rate Limited), 500 (Server Error)

exampleUseCase.title: Example: Telegram Bot with DataForge
exampleUseCase.content:
Let's walk through building a backend for a Telegram bot with DataForge step by step. This shows how all parts of the platform work together.

Task: a subscription management bot. Users can subscribe, check status, renew. Admin sees analytics.

Step 1 — Create a project:
Create a project named "Subscription Bot", slug: subscription-bot.

Step 2 — Create tables:
— users: telegram_id (bigint, unique), username (text), first_name (text), is_active (boolean, default: true)
— subscriptions: user_id (uuid, FK → users.id), plan (text), expires_at (timestamp), is_paid (boolean)
— payments: user_id (uuid, FK → users.id), amount (decimal), currency (text, default: 'RUB'), created_at (timestamp)

Step 3 — Create an API token:
Settings > Tokens > Create Token. Copy the token — you'll need it for bot authorization.

Step 4 — Integrate with the bot:
Open the SDK documentation. Ready-made Python examples:

import requests
API_URL = 'https://your-domain.com/api/v1/subscription-bot'
TOKEN = 'df_live_your_token_here'
headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {TOKEN}'}

# Create user
requests.post(f'{API_URL}/tables/users/rows', json={'telegram_id': 123456, 'username': 'john'}, headers=headers)

# Get user
requests.get(f'{API_URL}/tables/users/rows?filter[telegram_id]=123456', headers=headers)

Or simply show the documentation to an AI assistant (ChatGPT/Claude) and ask it to write a full bot — it will see all endpoints, data types, request formats and do everything for you.

Step 5 — Set up automation:
— Enable the "Webhooks" plugin and create a webhook on the payments table → send a Telegram notification on every new payment
— Enable the "Cron Jobs" plugin and create a job: check expired subscriptions every hour (UPDATE subscriptions SET is_paid = false WHERE expires_at < NOW())
— Enable the "Dashboards" plugin and create a panel: active subscription count, monthly revenue, new users this week

Step 6 — Monitoring:
Enable the "Analytics" plugin — monitor API request counts, response times, and popular endpoints.

Result: a full backend for a Telegram bot in 15 minutes. No servers, no hand-written APIs, no DevOps. Just data, logic, and automation.
